title: London - Brown Wolf
author: london
exclude_reason: Although set partly in California and referencing the Klondike frontier, this is a Jack London animal/literary story about a dog's loyalty, not a western. It lacks the defining elements of the western genre (cowboys, outlaws, settlers vs. frontier justice, American West gunplay); the core conflict is emotional and philosophical about a dog's choice between two masters.
summary: A poet and his wife in rural California have adopted a wolf-like Klondike dog they call Wolf; when his original owner, a Klondike musher named Skiff Miller, appears, they agree to let the dog choose, and the dog ultimately chooses to follow his old master back north.

title: London - A Day's Lodging
author: london
exclude_reason: This is a complete, well-formed Jack London story, but it is not a western. It is set in the Klondike/Yukon (Northland fiction) and centers on a psychological confrontation between a man, his estranged wife, and her lover—frontier survival and gold, but not the American West cowboys/outlaws/frontier-justice milieu that defines the western genre.
summary: John Messner, traveling the frozen Yukon, shares a cabin with a doctor and his companion—who turns out to be Messner's runaway wife and her lover. Messner coolly extorts the lover's entire gold sack as the price of leaving, then dumps the gold in the river, walking away liberated.

issues.description: The U+00A0 non-breaking spaces flagged in QA are benign typographic spacing from the HTML source and do not corrupt the text.

title: London - Love Of Life
author: london
exclude_reason: Jack London's 'Love of Life' is a Northland/Arctic wilderness survival tale set in the Canadian Barrens near the Arctic Ocean, not a frontier American West western. It lacks cowboys, outlaws, settlers, or frontier-justice elements; the genre claim is wrong.
summary: A gold prospector, deserted by his companion Bill in the Canadian Barrens, endures starvation and the elements while crawling toward a distant whale-ship, ultimately outlasting a sick wolf that stalks him and being rescued by a scientific expedition.

issues.description: The U+00A0 non-breaking spaces are ordinary typographic spacing artifacts from the source formatting; curly quotes and em-dashes are author-intentional period typography. Not corrupting.
Asterisk section-break markers (* * * * *) appear between scenes; these are the author's/edition's intended section dividers rather than stray artifacts.

title: London - Negore, the Coward
author: london
genre_suggestion: adventure / historical frontier fiction (Alaskan, not American West western)
summary: In Russian-occupied 1800s Alaska, Negore, branded a coward by his tribe and his beloved Oona, redeems himself by luring the Russian commander Ivan and his men into a deadly ambush, dying in Oona's arms as she finally names him a brave man.

issues.description: A section-break marker rendered as a line of asterisks ('* * * * *') separates the two halves of the story; benign but a formatting artifact.
Numerous U+00A0 non-breaking spaces appear throughout, likely from HTML extraction; they do not corrupt the text but are non-ASCII whitespace artifacts.

title: London - The Story Of Keesh
author: london
exclude_reason: The story is set in the Arctic among an Inuit tribe on the polar sea and concerns bear hunting and tribal politics; it is not a western (frontier American West, cowboys, outlaws, settlers). It is a complete, well-formed narrative, but the claimed genre is wrong.
summary: Keesh, a poor Inuit boy on the polar sea, becomes a renowned hunter and rises to head man of his village by using clever "headcraft"—coiled whalebone in frozen blubber balls—to kill polar bears rather than the witchcraft his people suspect.

issues.description: Numerous U+00A0 non-breaking spaces are present (flagged as double spaces after periods). These are typographic artifacts but do not impair readability.
